mappings.rationaleAI: Spontaneous nuclear transformation of unstable isotopes is the phenomenon of radioactivity (C.1). Alpha, beta, and gamma decay modes are types of radioactivity (C.2). Energy released as radiation addresses energy release associated with radioactive decay (C.5). Exponential decay statistics characterized by half-life address the exponential decay law (C.7.a), while alpha (C.7.b), beta (C.7.c), and gamma (C.7.d) decay are named decay rate categories. Applications including radiometric dating, medical isotopes, and radiation therapy address applications of radioactivity (C.8).

The weak interaction drives radioactive beta decay by transforming quarks between flavors, directly addressing beta decay rates (C.7.c). As one of the four fundamental forces it is a fundamental force associated with particle interactions (H.2). Electroweak unification (Glashow-Weinberg-Salam) describes relations of weak interactions to electromagnetic interactions (H.6).

Quantum field theory classifying all known elementary particles addresses the known elementary particles (H.1.d). Three fundamental interactions (electromagnetic, weak, strong) address forces associated with particle interactions (H.2). Classification of fermions and bosons addresses systems for classifying elementary particles (H.3). Symmetry breaking and conservation laws address elementary particles and conservation laws (H.4).

Particles smaller than an atom divided into composite (protons, neutrons) and elementary (electrons, quarks, photons) address the development of subatomic particle concepts (H.1) and the known elementary particles (H.1.d). Classification by composition, spin statistics, mass, and decay mode addresses systems for classifying elementary particles (H.3).

Studies of fundamental constituents of matter via the Standard Model framework address subatomic particles broadly (H). Fermions and bosons as matter and force particles (H.1). Four fundamental interactions (H.2). Three fermion generations and classification (H.3). Experimental accelerators (LHC) as sources of unstable elementary particles (H.5).

The strong nuclear force confining quarks into hadrons and binding nucleons into nuclei is a fundamental force associated with particle interactions (H.2). The residual nuclear force binding protons and neutrons addresses theories of nuclear structure and nuclear binding force (A.6). QCD as the theory of nuclear forces involving elementary particles (H.9).

The proton as a stable positively charged baryon is a component of atomic nuclei (A.2.b). Proton count defining atomic number addresses nuclear charge (A.1.b). Proton mass of ~1836 electron masses addresses nuclear mass properties (A.1.a).

The neutron as a component of the nucleus (A.2.a) with properties including mass, charge neutrality, and quark composition (D.1). Neutron sources from reactors and spallation (D.2). Nuclear reactions produced by neutrons including fission, fusion, and capture (D.4).

Atomic number (proton count) defining element identity addresses nuclear charge (A.1.b). Isotopes and nuclides address isotopes broadly (B, A.3) including classification of nuclides (B.1) and isotopic composition of elements (B.2). Origin of elements through stellar nucleosynthesis addresses formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8).

Atoms comprising a dense nucleus of protons and neutrons address the structure of the atomic nucleus (A) and general properties including mass and charge (A.1). Proton and neutron composition addresses components of atomic nuclei (A.2).

Hydrogen as the lightest element with a single proton nucleus addresses general properties of atomic nuclei at their simplest (A.1). As the primary fuel of stellar fusion, hydrogen connects to thermonuclear reactions in the Sun and stars (G.3.a).

Thermonuclear hydrogen-to-helium fusion in stellar cores addresses thermonuclear reactions in the Sun and stars (G.3.a). Stellar nucleosynthesis producing all elements heavier than lithium addresses formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8).
Nuclear fusion in the Sun's core converting hydrogen to helium directly addresses thermonuclear reactions in the Sun and the stars (G.3.a).

Sequential formation of subatomic particles and atoms from the initial hot dense state, including primordial element abundances (Big Bang nucleosynthesis), directly addresses the formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8).

Ionized gas with free electrons and ions, governed by magnetohydrodynamics, with applications in nuclear fusion reactors addresses basic conditions for a thermonuclear reactor (G.4), including formation of suitable plasma (G.4.a) and confinement and control of high-temperature plasma (G.4.b).

Mass as an intrinsic property of matter with E=mc^2 and the Brout-Englert-Higgs mechanism conferring particle masses addresses nuclear mass properties (A.1.a) and connects to known elementary particles and the Higgs boson (H.1.d).

Magnetic phenomena arising from intrinsic magnetic moments of elementary particles, including electron spin and orbital angular momentum, connect to nuclear magnetic moment and nuclear magnetic resonance phenomena (A.1.e).

Quantum theory governing matter at atomic and subatomic scales underpins nuclear models and properties of nuclear states (A.5). Wave functions, superposition, and uncertainty principles are the quantum mechanical symmetry operations related to subatomic particles (H.4.a). Quantum field theory provides theories and models of nuclear reactions (E.3).

Energy conservation laws and nuclear energy forms address energy relationships of nuclear reactions (E.2). Relativistic energy-mass equivalence (E=mc^2) connects to energy released in radioactive decay (C.5) and energy released in fission (F.1.c).

Massless gauge boson mediating electromagnetic force is a known elementary particle (H.1.d). High-energy photon release in gamma decay (C.7.d). Passage of electromagnetic waves and their interaction with atomic structure (I.1.a).

Self-propagating waves of coupled electric and magnetic fields including X-rays and gamma rays address the passage of electromagnetic waves and their interaction with atomic structure (I.1.a). Wavelength-dependent interaction with matter addresses passage of radiations through matter (I.1.b). Gamma rays as high-energy electromagnetic radiation connect to gamma decay (C.7.d).

Ordinary baryonic matter (atoms, protons, neutrons) and exotic forms (antimatter, quark-gluon plasma) address subatomic particles (H) and development of particle concepts (H.1). Interaction of particles with matter broadly addresses effects of passage of particles through matter (I).

Einstein's E=mc^2 from special relativity is fundamental to the energy relationships of nuclear reactions (E.2), establishing the equivalence of mass and energy that underpins all nuclear energy calculations.
Carbon isotope C-14 enabling radiocarbon dating is an application of radioactive and stable isotopes (B.6). Carbon isotopes broadly connect to the isotope framework (B).

Noether's theorem establishing the connection between continuous symmetry and conservation laws in physics directly underpins elementary particles and conservation-symmetry laws (H.4) and quantum mechanical symmetry operations related to subatomic particles (H.4.a).

Supernovae as the dominant source of elements from oxygen to rubidium address formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8). Core-collapse supernovae involve thermonuclear processes in massive stars (G.3.a).
Modeling the universe's origin and evolution including primordial nucleosynthesis and element formation addresses the formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8).

The quantum vacuum containing zero-point energy and virtual particle fluctuations (Casimir effect) connects to other particles suggested by contemporary theoretical ideas (H.7), as quantum vacuum phenomena inform particle physics beyond the Standard Model.
Nuclear explosives releasing energy suddenly connect to nuclear explosions and atomic bombs as an application of fission chain reactions reaching critical mass (F.2.a).

The universe's composition including ordinary baryonic matter, chronology from Big Bang through structure formation, and the LCDM model connect to the formation and evolution of atomic nuclei in the universe (A.8).

The four fundamental interactions — gravity, electromagnetism, strong nuclear, weak nuclear — are the fundamental forces associated with particle interactions (H.2). Nuclear forces binding nucleons address theories of nuclear structure and nuclear binding force (A.6).
